notes.anchor: the Nones of March, your birthday
notes.note: Atticus's birthday fell on the Nones of March (7 March); Cicero, isolated at Formiae, marks the date as he waits for a fuller letter. The personal touch sits oddly against the political crisis, which is exactly the contrast Cicero feels.

notes.anchor: that Gnaeus had embarked well attended and that the consuls had crossed over
notes.note: "Gnaeus" is Pompey the Great, named familiarly by praenomen throughout book 9. The two conditions Cicero recalls Atticus setting --- Pompey safely shipped and the consuls of 49 across the Adriatic --- were the trigger that would, in his mind, release him to leave Italy too.

The consuls of 49 BC, C. Claudius Marcellus and L. Cornelius Lentulus Crus, were the Republic's chief magistrates and had thrown in with Pompey; their crossing to Greece would mark the legitimate government's abandonment of Italy.

notes.anchor: From Brundisium nothing had yet been brought
notes.note: The recurring refrain of these days: no word yet from Brundisium, where Pompey was bottled up by Caesar's pursuing army. Cicero's every decision hangs on whether the escape succeeds.
